Name: logdet_curvature_routing
Title: Log-Determinant Curvature Routing: Scalable Precision Allocation via Tangent Hessian Geometry
Short Hypothesis: The log-determinant of tangent Hessians provides a scalable, curvature-aware proxy for precision sensitivity in neural network activations, enabling deterministic precision routing without costly calibration or training.
Related Work: Existing precision routing methods, such as Spectral Quantization Routing (SQR), rely on activation covariance geometry but do not explicitly incorporate curvature information. The Affine Normal Directions paper introduces log-determinant gradients as a scalable alternative to third-order derivatives, which inspires our approach. Our proposal uniquely bridges log-determinant geometry with precision routing, offering a mathematically transparent and deterministic framework for mixed-precision inference.
Abstract: Mixed-precision inference offers significant computational savings but remains challenging due to the lack of scalable, curvature-aware precision allocation mechanisms. We propose Log-Determinant Curvature Routing (LDCR), a training-free framework that dynamically allocates numerical precision based on the log-determinant of tangent Hessians. We hypothesize that the log-determinant provides a scalable proxy for curvature sensitivity, with low log-determinant values indicating robustness to low-precision computation. LDCR computes these geometric diagnostics using matrix-free tangent linear solves and Hessian-vector products, enforcing strict latency budgets through deterministic precision-switching policies. We ground our approach in PAC-Bayes bounds, linking log-determinant curvature to generalization stability under quantization noise. Evaluated on single-GPU setups across WILDS benchmarks, LDCR achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to static and heuristic mixed-precision baselines. By replacing qualitative fragility claims with formalized curvature diagnostics, LDCR provides a transparent, reproducible pathway for resource-efficient inference.
Experiments: Evaluate LDCR on WILDS benchmarks (e.g., Camelyon17, CivilComments) across diverse precision tiers (FP16, FP8, INT8), measuring accuracy retention and throughput improvement.
Compare LDCR against SQR and static mixed-precision baselines, using spectral decay rates and condition numbers as alternative proxies for precision sensitivity.
Validate the scalability of matrix-free log-determinant computation by measuring wall-clock latency and memory overhead across varying model sizes (e.g., GPT-2, ResNet-50).
Analyze the correlation between log-determinant values and quantization sensitivity by measuring the degradation in accuracy when switching precision tiers.
Provide theoretical PAC-Bayes bounds linking log-determinant curvature to generalization stability under quantization noise.
Perform ablation studies to isolate the impact of tangent linear solves and Hessian-vector products on diagnostic accuracy and computational overhead.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The matrix-free computation of log-determinants may introduce additional overhead for small models or shallow architectures.
The effectiveness of LDCR may degrade for highly non-convex loss landscapes, where tangent Hessians provide less reliable curvature information.
The deterministic precision-switching policy may struggle with highly dynamic input distributions, requiring adaptive threshold calibration.
Theoretical bounds may not fully capture practical quantization noise, necessitating empirical validation across diverse benchmarks.
